intro: April 1985. Pepsi is winning blind taste tests and gaining ground. Your researchers have a sweeter formula that beats both Pepsi and Coke in testing. You run Coca-Cola, and you're about to change a 99-year-old recipe that Americans consider almost sacred.

realOutcome.summary: New Coke was a legendary blunder — that the company recovered from spectacularly. Every 'what actually happened' below is real.

decisions.scenario: Blind tests say a sweeter formula wins. You can replace the classic Coca-Cola recipe with 'New Coke' — or trust that people drink the brand and the feeling, not just the sugar.

choices.text: Change the 99-year formula to New Coke.
choices.reveal: Coca-Cola reformulated to New Coke in 1985 based on taste tests. The tests measured sips, not loyalty — people didn't want a better cola, they wanted THEIR cola.

choices.text: Keep the classic formula and out-market Pepsi.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You compete on brand and emotion, where Coke is unbeatable, instead of on sweetness. No revolt, no crisis, just marketing muscle.

choices.text: Launch a sweeter flavor alongside the original.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You add a new option without killing the classic. You capture the sweet-tooth crowd and keep the loyalists — the win New Coke threw away.

decisions.scenario: To fully commit to New Coke, you'd pull original Coca-Cola from shelves entirely. There can only be one flagship.

choices.reveal: Coca-Cola pulled the original from the market. Outrage was immediate — protest groups formed, the company got 400,000 angry calls and letters, and people hoarded old Coke like treasure.

choices.text: Sell New Coke alongside the original.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You never take the original away, so there's nothing to revolt against. Two products quietly split the market instead of one igniting a rebellion.

choices.text: Test New Coke in a few cities first.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: A limited rollout reveals the loyalty problem before it's national. You catch the mistake in a test market, not on every front page in America.

decisions.scenario: The backlash is a national event. Talk shows, protests, a man collecting signatures to bring back the original. You can dig in and defend your decision, or reverse fast.

choices.reveal: After just 79 days, Coca-Cola brought back the original as 'Coca-Cola Classic.' The return was front-page news, sales surged past pre-New-Coke levels, and the blunder became a marketing gift.

choices.text: Dig in and defend New Coke to the end.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You mistake stubbornness for strength. Every week you defend New Coke, customers defect to Pepsi and to their own freezers full of the old stuff.

choices.text: Slowly phase the original back over a year.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: A cautious, drawn-out reversal robs you of the dramatic comeback moment. The apology loses its power when it arrives in slow motion.

decisions.scenario: The press wants to know what on earth happened. You can own the whole mess publicly, spin it as a secret master plan, or say nothing.

choices.text: Own it plainly: 'We're not that dumb, or that smart.'
choices.reveal: Coca-Cola's president Donald Keough disarmed the conspiracy theories with the famous line that the company was neither smart enough nor dumb enough to have planned it. Honesty turned humiliation into affection.

choices.text: Blame customers for not appreciating the new taste.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You tell your most loyal fans they have bad taste. Nothing rebuilds a boycott faster than a brand that lectures the people it just betrayed.

choices.text: Stay silent and hope it blows over.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: Silence lets the story write itself, and the story is that Coke is out of touch. A comeback needs a voice, not a hiding executive.

decisions.scenario: The crisis is behind you. Now the real question: what kind of company is Coca-Cola? You can press your growing wins — Diet Coke, bottler consolidation — or retreat to just protecting the flagship.

choices.text: Press the Diet Coke win and consolidate bottlers.
choices.reveal: Goizueta pressed forward — Diet Coke became a massive success, bottler consolidation boosted margins, and Coca-Cola's value grew from about $4B to $145B during his tenure.

choices.text: Retreat to protecting only flagship Coke.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: Shell-shocked by New Coke, you play defense forever. You keep the flagship safe and miss the growth that made Coca-Cola a global juggernaut.

choices.text: Chase unrelated diversification to feel safe.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You buy your way into businesses you don't understand instead of pressing the beverage empire you dominate. Distraction dressed up as prudence.

bands.line: You survived the blunder and grew an empire. Villain episode: WON.
Bruised but recovered. The flagship is safe; the ambition isn't.
You broke the most trusted brand in the world. Faithful to history's worst instincts. 💀

endings.ctaLine: Coke turned its worst blunder into a comeback. Today's agents flagged a brand trusting the spreadsheet over its fans →
